1: A good name is to be chosen over great riches. The NT's ultimate statement on the incomparable name comes in Philippians 2:9: God exalted Christ and gave him 'the name that is above every name.' The 'good name' that wisdom calls more valuable than silver is a shadow of the name of Jesus, which is the highest name in creation. Matthew 12:42 declares that 'something greater than Solomon' stands here — the one whose name surpasses all wisdom's endorsements.
2: The rich and the poor have this in common: the LORD made them both — the theological foundation for economic solidarity. The NT applies this impartiality directly to the community of Christ: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28). Peter declares that 'God shows no partiality' (Acts 10:34); the social barrier that divides rich and poor is dismantled in the community formed by the one who 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor' (2 Corinthians 8:9).
3: The prudent person sees danger and takes cover; the naive go on and suffer. Discernment — perceiving the threat before it arrives — is a gift of wisdom. The NT applies this to the disciple's vigilance: 'Be sober-minded; be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' (1 Peter 5:8). The cover that wisdom provides is the protection of abiding in Christ: 'Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit' (John 15:5).
4: The reward of humility and the fear of the LORD is riches, honor, and life — the paradox at the heart of wisdom: descent leads to ascent. This is the precise pattern of Christ's incarnation and exaltation: 'He humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Therefore God has highly exalted him' (Philippians 2:8-9). Proverbs 22:4 is the structural principle of which the crucifixion-resurrection is the supreme enactment. Humility before God leads to the life and honor that only God can give.
5: Thorns and snares are in the path of the perverse — the imagery of thorns is loaded with the curse of Genesis 3:18: 'thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you.' The path of the perverse is the cursed ground of the fallen world. The christological fulfillment is the crown of thorns (Matthew 27:29; Mark 15:17; John 19:2): Christ bore the thorns of the curse on his head, absorbing into himself the very emblem of the Fall. The snare in the way of the perverse is what Christ entered in order to dismantle: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13).
6: Train a child in the way he should go; when he is old he will not depart from it — the wisdom of early formation. The NT picture of God's own child-formation runs through the incarnation: Luke records that 'Jesus increased in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man' (Luke 2:52), growing up in the formation of a devout Jewish family. More broadly, God trains his children through discipline (Hebrews 12:7-10): 'He disciplines us for our good, that we may share his holiness.' Formation by the Father is the pattern Christ both modeled and secured for his people.
7: The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is slave to the lender — a social observation without explicit moral comment, but carrying implicit prophetic weight. The NT's reversal: 'For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery' (Galatians 5:1). The debt-slavery metaphor runs through the NT's understanding of sin: we were debtors (Matthew 6:12: forgive us our debts), and Christ paid what we could not. The economic observation of Proverbs 22:7 finds its theological extension in the liberation Christ accomplished.
8: Whoever sows injustice will reap trouble — the harvest principle. Paul cites the same structure in Galatians 6:7-8: 'Do not be deceived: God is not mocked, for whatever one sows, that will he also reap. For the one who sows to his own flesh will from the flesh reap corruption, but the one who sows to the Spirit will from the Spirit reap eternal life.' The sowing-reaping principle of Proverbs is made eschatological in Paul: the harvest of injustice is not merely social trouble but eternal corruption, reversed only by sowing to the Spirit of Christ.
9: A generous person will be blessed, for he shares his bread with the poor — the blessedness of generosity. Paul applies this in 2 Corinthians 9:6-9, quoting Psalms 112:9 but assuming the Proverbs framework: 'Whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly.' The christological foundation for generosity is the incarnation: 'You know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9). Sharing bread with the poor is participating in the pattern of Christ.
10: Drive out the mocker and conflict goes with him — the purging of divisiveness from the community. The NT applies a similar principle to the church: 'A little leaven leavens the whole lump' (1 Corinthians 5:6); the one who causes divisions is to be avoided after a first and second warning (Titus 3:10). The mocker whose removal brings peace anticipates the eschatological removal of all that corrupts: 'in him there is no sin' (1 John 3:5), and his kingdom is the community from which all mockery is finally removed (Revelation 21:27).
11: Whoever loves a pure heart and gracious speech will have the king as his friend — the connection between character and royal favor. The NT's application: 'Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God' (Matthew 5:8). Paul calls for speech that is 'gracious, seasoned with salt' (Colossians 4:6). The king as friend — the ultimate social endorsement — points to Christ's invitation: 'No longer do I call you servants... but I have called you friends' (John 15:15). Purity of heart and gracious speech are the character traits of the Christ-follower who is befriended by the King of kings.
12: The eyes of the LORD watch over knowledge and frustrate the words of the treacherous — divine omniscience as both protection and judgment. The NT: 'No creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account' (Hebrews 4:13). Christ himself 'did not need anyone to testify about man, for he himself knew what was in man' (John 2:25). The watching eyes of the LORD that protect knowledge and expose treachery are the eyes of the risen Christ who walks among the lampstands (Revelation 2:18: his eyes are like a flame of fire).
13: The sluggard says, 'There is a lion outside! I will be killed in the streets!' — fear as an excuse for inaction. The NT diagnoses the same pattern: the one-talent servant buried his talent 'because I was afraid' (Matthew 25:25). The master's response — 'You wicked and slothful servant!' — identifies fear-based inaction as a form of wickedness. Christ calls his disciples to courage: 'Do not be afraid, little flock, for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom' (Luke 12:32). The spirit of fear that manufactures excuses is countered by the Spirit of adoption that casts out fear (2 Timothy 1:7).
14: The mouth of an adulterous woman is a deep pit — the covenant-infidelity that leads to death. The NT extends the spiritual dimension: James warns that 'friendship with the world is enmity with God' (James 4:4) and Paul uses the adultery metaphor for spiritual unfaithfulness (2 Corinthians 11:2-3). The pit of spiritual death is what Christ descended into and rose from; the deep pit of the adulteress is the structural opposite of the life that Christ offers: 'I came that they may have life and have it abundantly' (John 10:10).
15: Foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child, but the rod of discipline will drive it far — the reality of human moral default. The NT diagnosis is categorical: 'None is righteous, no, not one' (Romans 3:10); 'all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God' (Romans 3:23). The bound-up folly that requires discipline points to the need not merely for correction but for new birth: 'You must be born again' (John 3:7). The rod that drives out folly is the discipline of God through Christ's word and Spirit — or, in the deepest sense, the cross, which bears the wages of folly to bring freedom from it.
16: Oppressing the poor to enrich yourself, or giving gifts to the wealthy — both lead only to poverty — the double condemnation of social injustice. The Magnificat (Luke 1:52-53) declares the reversal: 'He has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent empty away.' Both directions of injustice — exploitation and flattery of the powerful — are judged and inverted in the kingdom that Christ inaugurates.
17: Turn your ear and listen to the words of the wise — the opening of the 'Thirty Sayings' collection (Proverbs 22:17-24:22) with a direct appeal to the student. The appeal to listen is the NT's own call to discipleship: 'He who has ears to hear, let him hear' (Matthew 13:9). Christ is the wisdom-teacher par excellence who says 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden' (Matthew 11:28) — the invitation to wisdom made personal and urgent.
18: It is good to keep them in your heart and have them ready on your lips — the internalization of wisdom as the basis for its expression. The Deuteronomic parallel (Deuteronomy 6:6-7: these words shall be on your heart; you shall teach them) is fulfilled in the new covenant: 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts' (Jeremiah 31:33). Christ is the mediator of this new covenant (Hebrews 9:15), making the internalization that Proverbs calls for possible through the Spirit who dwells in believers.
19: I am teaching you today — you specifically — so that your confidence may be in the LORD — wisdom addressed to the individual student. The personal address echoes throughout Jesus's ministry: 'Follow me' (Matthew 4:19) — addressed to specific individuals. The confidence that comes from wisdom is not self-confidence but theocentric trust; Paul echoes this exactly: 'I have learned, in whatever situation I am, to be content' — confidence not from circumstances but from Christ who strengthens (Philippians 4:11-13).
20: Have I not written down for you thirty excellent sayings of counsel and knowledge — the appeal to a written, authoritative source of wisdom. The NT's parallel: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching' (2 Timothy 3:16). The written wisdom of Proverbs is part of the Scripture that Christ 'fulfills' (Matthew 5:17) and that points to him (John 5:39: it is they that bear witness about me). The thirty sayings as authoritative counsel anticipates the authoritative teaching of Jesus who speaks 'as one who had authority, and not as their scribes' (Matthew 7:29).
21: To teach you true and reliable words, so that you may give an accurate answer to those who sent you — the epistemic purpose of wisdom. The NT applies this to the disciple's witness: 'always being prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you' (1 Peter 3:15). Jesus himself is 'the truth' (John 14:6), and those formed by wisdom about him are equipped to give 'accurate answers' — the verbal form of the truth they have received.
22: Do not rob the poor because they are poor or crush the afflicted at the city gate — the prophetic ethics of covenant protection for the vulnerable. The prophets amplified this: Isaiah 1:17 (seek justice, correct oppression, defend the fatherless). Jesus embodies and enacts it: 'Blessed are the poor in spirit' (Matthew 5:3); 'the Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to proclaim good news to the poor' (Luke 4:18). Oppression of the poor is not merely social injustice but an offense against the one who identifies with the poor and marginalized.
23: For the LORD will take up their case and will plunder those who plundered them — the divine advocacy for the oppressed. The parable of the unjust judge (Luke 18:1-8) engages this promise directly: 'Will not God give justice to his elect, who cry to him day and night?' (Luke 18:7). Christ is the ultimate Advocate (1 John 2:1: we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous) who takes up the case of the oppressed — and his advocacy is grounded in his own experience of being handed over to unjust power.
24: Do not befriend an angry man or associate with someone hot-tempered — the power of companionship to form character. The NT's positive form: 'Do not be deceived: Bad company ruins good morals' (1 Corinthians 15:33). Jesus's choice of companions is significant: he ate with tax collectors and sinners not to be formed by their character but to offer transformation. The disciples he called — 'Follow me' — were formed by his company, not by the company of the proud and violent. Companionship with Christ is the alternative wisdom offers.
25: Lest you learn his ways and set a trap for yourself — the insidious nature of moral contagion. The NT's 'be transformed by the renewing of your mind' (Romans 12:2) addresses the same reality from the positive direction: formation through companionship either corrupts or transforms depending on the company. The trap that angry company sets for the naive is the trap from which Christ frees — 'if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed' (John 8:36).
26: Do not be one of those who shakes hands in a pledge or puts up security for loans — the wisdom of not taking on the debts of another impulsively. This is practical wisdom about financial exposure, but the christological irony is total: Christ is the one who did exactly what Proverbs warns against — he became surety for our debt, pledged himself for our obligations, and paid what we owed (Hebrews 7:22: Jesus has become the guarantor of a better covenant). What wisdom forbids as foolishness for a human becomes the gracious act of the mediator.
27: If you have nothing to pay with, why let him take away your bed from under you? — the realistic appraisal of the cost of surety. The wisdom principle stands: do not over-extend. But again the christological reversal: Christ was stripped (John 19:24: they divided his garments), left with nothing, so that we who had nothing might receive the wealth of his inheritance. He was impoverished so that we might be rich (2 Corinthians 8:9). What Proverbs warns the wise to avoid, Christ embraced voluntarily for our sake.
28: Do not move the ancient boundary stone that your ancestors put in place — the permanence of the covenant structure established by previous generations. The law as boundary is permanent: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17). Christ does not remove the ancient markers; he fulfills them from within. The boundary stones of covenant ethics stand; Christ is the one who embodies and completes what they delimit.
29: Do you see a man skilled in his work? He will serve kings — not obscure people — the excellence and service principle. The faithful servant who is skilled in his work is given greater responsibility: 'Well done, good and faithful servant. You have been faithful over a little; I will set you over much' (Matthew 25:21). The skilled servant who stands before kings anticipates the disciples who will judge the twelve tribes (Matthew 19:28) and the saints who will reign with Christ (Revelation 20:4). Excellence in service leads to royal commission — the pattern Christ establishes.
